one_line_takeaway: MMA-RAGT adds a stateful, belief-tracking LLM trust agent (interpreting agentic RAG defense as a POMDP) at 5 pipeline checkpoints, cutting attack success rate 6.50x on average across 43,774 adversarial instances with negligible utility loss, but at 3.34x latency cost and with an admitted semantic blind spot on tool-flip attacks (only 1.29x reduction).
key_finding: MMA-RAGT reduces ASR by an average factor of 6.50x across the five ART-SafeBench threat surfaces relative to the undefended baseline, with negligible utility cost (CR 0.87 vs baseline 0.88; AR 0.93 vs baseline 0.93 on Natural Questions). Per-surface ASR (baseline% -> defended%, reduction factor), all with GPT-4o backbone: B1 Text poisoning 76.5->15.7 (4.87x); B2a Image/OCR 22.3->2.0 (11.15x); B2b Image/multimodal 10.1->5.5 (1.84x); B3 Direct query 59.0->4.1 (14.39x); B4 Tool-flip 82.1->63.5 (1.29x, the weakest result); B5 Agentic integrity 48.5->8.9 (5.45x). The 2x2 factorial ablation on B1 (Table 3) shows: no-MTA baseline 76.5% ASR; stateless multi-stage (models NeMo Guardrails-style multi-point filtering) 56.7% (reference); stateful multi-stage (full MMA-RAGT) 30.3%, a 26.4 pp gain attributed to statefulness/belief tracking; stateful final-only 43.9%, giving a 13.6 pp gap between stateful final-only and stateful multi-stage attributed to spatial coverage; the stateless final-only cell is not independently measured but inferred (marked with a dagger) to equal the stateless multi-stage value under the paper's Proposition 2(i) high-correlation argument. Cross-LLM generalization on B1 (Table 4/Appendix D): baseline ASR ranges 70.9% (Llama-3.3-70B) to 78.4% (GPT-4o-mini); defended ASR ranges 7.5% (Llama-3.3-70B) to 31.5% (GPT-4o-mini), yielding 2.5x-9.5x reduction factors, with B4-hardest/B3-easiest ordering invariant across backbones. Per-OWASP breakdown for B3 (n=4,978 of 10,005, Table 5): LLM02 (sensitive info disclosure) and LLM03 (supply chain) driven to 0%; LLM05 (insecure output handling) to <1%; LLM01 (prompt injection) 64%->8%; LLM10 (model DoS) 61%->7%; LLM09 (misinfo/harmful) 63%->5%. Cost: mean end-to-end latency rises from 2.59+/-0.77s to 8.64+/-2.92s (3.34x overhead), with an estimated ~3,500-5,000 additional input tokens and ~800-1,200 additional output tokens per query for the 5-checkpoint configuration.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not related work) is MMA-RAGT, an inference-time security overlay for multimodal agentic RAG built around a Modular Trust Agent (MTA). The MTA is a model-agnostic, isolated LLM-based mediator that intercepts artifacts at a configurable set of internal checkpoints (the paper instantiates C1 Query Screening, C2 Action Validation, C3 Retrieval Screening, C4 Tool Output Screening, C5 Response Verification) and maintains a natural-language 'belief state' Phi_t across checkpoints, updated as Phi_t = f_theta(Phi_t-1, o_t) via a single frozen LLM call, rather than treating each checkpoint independently. The authors formalize the defense problem as a Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (adversarial intent as latent variable Sadv) and derive two propositions: (1) belief-conditioned policies weakly dominate stateless policies and can strictly dominate under partial observability (Proposition 1, 'Strict Value of Memory'); (2) homogeneous stateless detectors applied at multiple checkpoints can collapse to zero marginal benefit when detection events are perfectly correlated across checkpoints, whereas a belief-conditioned detector can still gain from additional checkpoints (Proposition 2). These propositions are then empirically validated via a 2x2 factorial ablation on the B1 (text poisoning) benchmark rather than merely asserted.
threat_model: Defined explicitly in Section 2. System is modeled as a tuple (A_main orchestrator, K knowledge store, T tools, L_gen generator) mediated by an independent trust agent A_trust; execution is a trajectory over state/action spaces. The adversary A_adv targets OWASP-aligned objectives: (O1) harmful outputs, (O2) control subversion, (O3) exfiltration, (O4) resource exhaustion, with either black-box (Gamma_BB) or partial-access (Gamma_PA, poisoning a subset of K) capability, without white-box visibility into A_trust or model weights. Adversarial artifacts can enter via inputs (I), storage (K), tools (T), or inter-component channels (IC), giving three attack scenarios: S1 retrieval manipulation (poisoned K content surfacing via semantic similarity), S2 directive injection (inputs overriding system instructions), and S3 state corruption (inputs biasing the orchestrator toward tool misuse/drift). The key assumption motivating the whole design is that adversarial intent is a latent variable and sophisticated adversaries can chain S1-S3 across pipeline stages so no single observation reveals the composite attack -- rendering single-checkpoint/stateless defenses structurally inadequate. Defender assumes A_trust executes in a protected environment isolated from A_main to decorrelate failure modes (Section 3.5: execution isolation, model heterogeneity option, immutable audit logs).
limitations: Stated explicitly by the authors in the Limitations subsection and Discussion: (1) ASR judging relies on a deterministic GPT-4o judge, so evaluator bias may remain despite >95% manual agreement (only 85% agreement on B3 due to subjective bias-category ambiguity); (2) Tool-Flip attacks (B4) expose a 'semantic resolution limit' -- text-based belief tracking achieves only 1.29x reduction (82.1%->63.5%) because adversarial and benign tool-selection trajectories can be observationally indistinguishable to a semantic reasoner, and the authors state this requires complementary deterministic controls (tool allowlists, argument-schema validation) rather than belief tracking; (3) B2b (direct multimodal image injection, 1.84x) is harder than B2a (OCR-mediated, 11.15x) because visual injections can bypass the symbolic/text-based reasoning available to the trust policy -- a 'perceptual boundary'; (4) the overlay adds 3.34x latency overhead and the paper does not evaluate adaptive checkpoint scheduling or parallelization as mitigations (proposed but unvalidated in Appendix I); (5) as a fixed policy, MMA-RAGT may be vulnerable to white-box prompt optimization and recursive injection against the MTA itself; (6) other orchestration/memory designs than the evaluated ReAct+LangGraph instantiation may require different checkpoint placements -- the C1-C5 set is presented as one representative instantiation, not a universal architecture; (7) the stateless-final-only ablation cell in Table 3 is not independently measured, only inferred from Proposition 2(i), which is a gap in the empirical validation of the factorial design.

datasets_used: ART-SafeBench (Singh et al. 2025, the authors' own prior benchmark): 43,774 validated adversarial instances across B1 Text Poisoning (n=10,943), B2 Image Poisoning (n=4,000; B2a OCR-mediated, B2b direct multimodal), B3 Direct Query (n=10,005), B4 Tool-Flip (n=14,400), B5 Agentic Integrity (n=4,426)
Natural Questions dev set (Kwiatkowski et al. 2019), n=3,610, used for utility/relevance evaluation via the ARES framework
quantitative_results: Average ASR reduction factor across 5 threat surfaces: 6.50x
B1 Text poisoning ASR: 76.5% baseline -> 15.7% defended (4.87x)
Utility (Natural Questions, ARES): baseline CR=0.88/AR=0.93 vs MMA-RAGT CR=0.87/AR=0.93

B1 factorial ablation: no-MTA 76.5%; stateless multi-stage 56.7% (reference); stateful final-only 43.9% (-12.8pp vs no-MTA is not the comparison used; delta vs reference is stated); stateful multi-stage 30.3% (-26.4pp vs stateless multi-stage from statefulness; -13.6pp from stateful final-only to stateful multi-stage attributed to spatial coverage)

B3 per-OWASP ASR (n=4,978 of 10,005): LLM01 prompt injection 64%->8%; LLM05 insecure output handling 71%-> <1%; LLM02 sensitive info 36%->0%; LLM03 supply chain 58%->0%; LLM09 misinfo/harmful 63%->5%; LLM10 model DoS 61%->7%
Latency: 2.59+/-0.77s baseline -> 8.64+/-2.92s with MTA (3.34x overhead), ~3,500-5,000 extra input tokens and ~800-1,200 extra output tokens per query
Automated GPT-4o judge validated against 750 manually audited instances: >95% overall agreement (100% on B1/B2/B4/B5, 85% on B3)
baselines_compared: Undefended agentic RAG pipeline (identical architecture minus the MTA) -- the paper's primary comparison point for ASR reduction
Stateless final-only detector (models boundary classifiers such as Llama Guard) -- ablation cell, value inferred not independently measured
Stateless multi-stage detector (models multi-point guardrails such as NeMo Guardrails) -- ablation cell, empirically measured
ColBERTv2 + GPT-4 and DPR + BART (established ARES utility baselines from Saad-Falcon et al. 2023, included for reference only, not run by this paper's authors)
TrustAgent (Hua et al. 2024) discussed in Related Work as the closest prior system but noted as lacking a belief state -- not run as an empirical baseline in this paper's own experiments
